data.title: Goodbye Growth? Here's What I'm Buying As Value Mounts A Comeback
data.description:
Capital investment from the technology sector has not been this high since 2000. So far, this has been a mostly circular flow of spending within the tech space in a symbiotic relationship between hardware and software players.

That makes it concerning to learn of a recent study finding zero return on investment from the vast majority of organizations implementing artificial intelligence tools.

For personal use, we all know how helpful and productivity-enhancing AI can be. But on an enterprise basis, successful integration into workflows has apparently been harder than one would think.

That raises an ominous set of questions: Aren't enterprises supposed to be the primary customer for the legion of emerging AI tools coming to market? And if enterprise adoption of AI happens slower and more gradually than the market thinks, who is ultimately paying for all this AI investment?

These hanging questions go a long way in explaining techy-growth stocks' slide last week. (Last minute addition: Fed chairman Jay Powell's Jackson Hole speech seems to have swept all this concern about enterprise AI implementation under the rug, for now.)

And as Big Tech retrenched a bit, value-oriented dividend stocks have shone, proving their defensiveness and resilience.

Is this a blip or the start of a new trend? Only time will tell.

Here's the agenda for this week:

  • Diving into a troubling MIT study and exploring how the use of AI, however profitable, has already impacted the labor market.
  • A reminder of just how richly valued and complacent the market, especially some segments of it, appears to be right now. Credit spreads are ultra-tight, and a huge percentage of stock buybacks are non-accretive. Could value's rebound have already begun?
  • This dividend growth investor's three capital allocation priorities right now.

Onward!

The AI Profit Mirage?

A recent MIT study into the profitability impact of AI usage found that, among hundreds of organizations that collectively spent $30-40 billion on AI implementation, a whopping 95% of users saw zero returns on their investment.

To quote a Fortune article on the report:

Despite the rush to integrate powerful new models, about 5% of AI pilot programs achieve rapid revenue acceleration; the vast majority stall, delivering little to no measurable impact on P&L. The research—based on 150 interviews with leaders, a survey of 350 employees, and an analysis of 300 public AI deployments—paints a clear divide between success stories and stalled projects.

This isn't to say that companies won't keep trying to integrate AI tools, nor that these AI tools won't have a significant impact on productivity and labor markets over time.

In fact, there are already signs of labor market impacts. Quoting Fortune again:

Workforce disruption is already underway, especially in customer support and administrative roles. Rather than mass layoffs, companies are increasingly not backfilling positions as they become vacant. Most changes are concentrated in jobs previously outsourced due to their perceived low value.

This goes a long way in explaining why the unemployment rate remains so low despite weak job growth in recent months. As Baby Boomers retire and leave the labor force (especially those in administrative or "back office" roles), companies simply aren't hiring anyone to replace them. Thus, the labor force participation rate has dropped even as the unemployment rate has stayed rangebound.

If the labor force participation rate had stayed rangebound as well (that is, if job leavers went on to look for another job rather than retiring), then the unemployment rate would have surged to almost 5% at this point.

Clearly, AI is replacing some human labor by making workers more efficient. But that does not necessarily mean that paid versions of AI tools that enterprises are adopting and pushing on their workers are generating strong returns on investment.

It may simply indicate that the free versions of AI tools like ChatGPT or Gemini are being used in individualized ways by workers to make themselves more productive.

In other words, most of the productivity benefits from AI appear to come from free or low-cost chatbots and tools that workers use on an individualized basis, not the expensive, specialized, embedded applications that enterprises are trying to integrate into team workflows.

But if that is the case, and if enterprises pull back on AI application investment accordingly, then who will ultimately fund all of the capex being done in this space?

In Q2 2025, tech capex contributed about half a point to real GDP growth, about as much as consumer spending.

To my admittedly techno-illiterate eye, this MIT study looks like bad news for the biggest spenders on AI infrastructure and software development.

It does not mean that AI is all hype, but it may mean that commercial uses of AI at the enterprise level could be more limited and gradually implemented than is currently priced into tech stocks.

Market Maximalism and Value's Vengeance

When it comes to stock price performance, it's all about expectations versus reality. Sometimes real results are unimpressive, but expectations weren't that high either, and the stock price is therefore unaffected.

But in our current situation, expectations appear to be sky-high, which means that real results (especially for AI-related tech stocks) need to be extraordinarily good to justify expectations. So far, that seems to be the case. But so far, most tech companies' AI profits have come from the aforementioned circular flow of spending within the tech space.

Note that the forward price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 (SPY) has been at or above its current level only twice in the last three decades: During the Dot Com bubble of the late 1990s and during the euphoric stimulus-fueled rally of 2020-2021.

In both cases, reality could not live up to expectations. In both cases, valuations eventually declined as the market dialed back its optimism from nosebleed levels.

If the reality of commercial applications of AI does not live up to its high expectations, it seems likely that the same pattern will play out this time as well.

Take one obvious example: Palantir Technologies (PLTR), the software developer that primarily works with the US government in various capacities. The company's growth runway is undoubtedly deep and robust, and it boasts the leading market position in its space.

And yet, the valuation appears to defy all logic or grounding in reality. PLTR's price to sales ratio jumped over the last few years from the teens to over 100x recently.

The P/E ratio currently sits above 240x.

Now, I don't doubt that PLTR is a highly valuable company doing highly valuable work. But to my (again, admittedly techno-illiterate) eye, it appears as though momentum has carried the valuation of this great company up and up into the heavenly realm of absurdities.

If stock valuations were inadequately pricing in strong fundamentals, you would expect to see company insiders tacitly signaling that by increasing their open-market purchases of company stock.

We are not seeing that today. In fact, insider buying is around the lowest it typically gets right now.

Of course, if stocks are fairly or excessively valued, this is exactly what you would expect to see from insiders.

Instead of insiders using their own cash for stock purchases, they are largely instead using company cash to do more and more buybacks. Perhaps unsurprisingly, this year is expected to see a new record for US stock buybacks.

The interesting thing is that buybacks are overwhelmingly concentrated among the biggest and often most richly valued companies on the market.

Take, for example, a company that shifted from being against buybacks to becoming the poster child for regular buybacks: Apple (AAPL). Since 2013, AAPL has continuously repurchased stock, quarter after quarter, retiring over 40% of shares outstanding in the last 12 years.

When AAPL began its buyback program, the stock's trailing twelve month P/E ratio hung around 10x. But as AAPL's P/E ratio has gone higher and higher over the last decade, the buybacks have continued, slowing only slightly.

Perhaps it made sense for AAPL to do buybacks at 10x earnings, but does it make sense to keep doing buybacks at today's ~30x forward P/E ratio?

Miller/Howard Investments recently put out a research report on S&P 500 buybacks versus dividends. Strikingly, they found that the share of non-accretive buybacks (that do not increase EPS) has surged in the last few years to its highest level on record — higher even than the Dot Com bubble era of the late 1990s.

If done at a sufficiently high valuation, stock buybacks cease to be accretive. As a share of market capitalization, non-accretive buybacks are at record levels right now.

Here is one more sign of market exuberance: credit spreads between high-yield/junk bonds and their US government equivalents. When the spread is wide, it indicates market panic and weak performance. Junk bond defaults spike, which decreases their appeal to investors. When the spread is narrow, it indicates market complacency and strong corporate bond performance.

Right now, credit spreads are at their tightest level since 2007.

I wouldn't necessarily interpret this as a sign of an impending market crash or recession, but it does tend to happen during the late stage of economic cycles.

All of the above makes me wonder if we are on or near the precipice of a major market rotation from growth to value. Of course, there have been many false starts in value's comeback against growth over the last decade and a half, so it would be fair to say that the long-running techy-growth outperformance over value is probably not over.

Even so, it's interesting to note that since the release of the MIT study on commercial AI profitability on August 18th, value-oriented stocks such as those in the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) have outperformed techy-growth names such as those in the Nasdaq Index (QQQ) and Roundhill Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT):

Then on Friday, Jay Powell's dovish speech at Jackson Hole acted as a rising tide that lifted all boats in the stock market.

We'll have to watch and see if value's comeback is sustainable or merely a blip.

What I'm Buying Today

Powell's dovish Jackson Hole speech triggered a market-wide rally that took several individual stocks off my buy list, but there are still a handful of compelling opportunities among REITs.

My capital allocation priorities this week are as follows (from highest to lowest):

  1. Top up my positions in the individual REITs on my buy list.
  2. Build positions in the Brookfield baby bonds below.
  3. Resume building up my cash position.
Securities Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Brookfield BRP Holdings 4.625% Sub Notes (BEPH) 7.0% N/A
BIP Bermuda Holdings I 5.125% Sub Notes (BIPI) 7.2% N/A
Brookfield Finance I Plc 4.5% Sub Notes (BNJ) 7.1% N/A
EastGroup Properties (EGP) 3.3% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Iron Mountain (IRM) 3.3% High-Single-Digit to Low-Double-Digit
Cash ETF
iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH) 4.5% N/A

The REITs

The Fed's signaling of imminent rate cuts may be just what the doctor ordered to rejuvenate real estate investment trusts ("REITs").

Note that after the MIT study's release on August 18th and especially after Powell's Jackson Hole speech on August 22nd, the real estate sector (VNQ) has been rebounding with vigor relative to the SPY:

REITs typically have very little floating rate debt, and thus Fed rate cuts have little direct impact on most REITs' balance sheets.

However, there is a second-order impact of a lower Fed Funds Rate. Total assets in money market funds has surged over the last few years to around $7.2 trillion, having been drawn in by the inflation-beating 4%+ yields.

As the FFR drops over the next year or so, income-generating equities like REITs will become increasingly attractive relative to money market funds and short-term bonds. Fund flows are likely to incrementally shift from these safer income sources to REITs and other higher-yielding stocks.

If the FFR drops significantly enough, then there may eventually be some fund outflows from money market funds and into REITs and other dividend stocks, but for the foreseeable future, the shifting flows are more likely to be incremental.

But even an incremental shift could be hugely bullish for REITs. According to NAREIT, the total market capitalization of all US equity REITs was about $1.4 trillion in 2024. Compare that to the roughly $1 trillion in funds that have flowed into MMFs over the last year.

If even a fraction of fund flows into MMFs are diverted into REITs, then REITs should enjoy a robust bull market going forward.

As of Friday, August 22nd, that shift already appears to be underway, and I think the tailwind for dividend stocks should last for a year or more.

The defensiveness of EGP's last-mile industrial facilities in the Sunbelt, the recession-resistant and inflation-resistant nature of ELS's core manufactured housing portfolio, and IRM's strong growth pipeline of data centers continue to look appealing to me, and I hope to top up my positions in these REITs while I still can at reasonable prices.

The Brookfield Baby Bonds

The three subordinated notes securities listed in the table above are issues of various subsidiaries of Brookfield Asset Management (BAM), one of the largest global asset managers in the world.

Brookfield is a complex financial machine, which causes many investors to steer clear of its securities. For most investors, that is probably a reasonable conclusion. Perhaps I am blinded by hubris, but I do believe I understand these securities well enough to include them within my buyable universe.

Each of these securities are subordinated notes, meaning that they are senior to common and preferred stock but junior to secured debt, of which Brookfield's subsidiaries make liberal use.

So, while the massive investment grade status of Brookfield reduces risk, the perpetually large amount of secured debt that's senior to these securities increases risk.

The fact that these baby bonds sport coupon yields of 4.5% to 5.125% makes it highly unlikely that they will be called anytime soon.

BIPI is backed by Brookfield Infrastructure (BIP, BIPC) and pays interest in March, June, September, and December.

BEPH is backed by Brookfield Renewable (BEP, BEPC) and pays interest in January, April, July, and October.

BNJ is backed by Brookfield International Corporate Finance Limited, which is a subsidiary of Brookfield Corporation (BN), the parent company of the whole Brookfield ecosystem. BNJ pays interest in February, May, August, and November.

Thus, owning all three of these securities will result in interest payments every month of the year.

To be clear, the complexity of Brookfield's ecosystem is a risk, in my view. That is why I will keep these baby bond positions relatively small ones in my portfolio. But at 7%+ yields, they also make for attractive income sources.

The Cash Equivalent ETF

ICSH owns a variety of conservative, ultra-short-duration income assets such as corporate bonds, Treasuries, and certificates of deposit, and its expense ratio is quite low at 0.08%.

With both high-growth tech stocks and value-oriented dividend stocks rallying on the back of impending Fed rate cuts, allocating the incremental dollar to stocks is becoming less attractive to me.

In my reckoning, as market complacency and valuations rise, so too should my cash position.

As such, this week, I hope to have at least a little bit of incoming cash left over to put into ICSH.

data.title: 10 Best CEFs This Month: Average Yield Of Nearly 9% (August 2025)
data.description:
Note: We are changing the format of this monthly article from 5 Best CEFs to 10 Best CEFs. This will provide more flexibility to readers to choose the funds that suit their individual needs and risk tolerance. Also, the focus of the selections will not be on the current valuation so much, but on other attributes that reflect the best value for the long term.

===

Introduction:

The S&P500 has been making new all-time highs, almost on a regular basis. However, so much of the gains have been concentrated in the large technology and high-growth names. So, obviously, the closed-end funds that have exposure to equities of high-growth and technology names have done better than others. But at the same time, some other sectors have also performed well, such as Utility funds, MLPs, and some foreign funds such as Taiwan Fund (TWN) and Mexico Fund (MXF). Also, the renewed expectations of interest rate cuts, the funds that are likely to benefit from declining interest rates are in focus.

All that being said, it is hard to find anything that has both the quality and the low valuation, so buying the best of the income investments regularly in small quantities is the key. As always, investors have to tread carefully amid all the uncertainties.

Why CEFs?

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal level of yield of less than 1.20%. Income investors and retirees can't survive on that level of income unless they have a very large pool of capital. Alternatively, one can find solid blue-chip individual dividend stocks that pay much higher dividends than the S&P 500. They can also provide market-matching growth on a longer-term basis. To identify such stocks, we also publish a monthly article on the '5 Best DGI stocks'. You can find the most recent one here.

However, with CEFs, we can take the income to the next level. That said, they generally come with higher risks (discussed later), so we believe that an allocation of 20% to 30% to a diversified CEF portfolio should be good enough for most income investors. In summary, these investments should be used primarily for income, not growth.

We publish this monthly article on CEFs (Closed-end funds) to provide our readers with a select list of 10 CEFs that, as a group, provide wide diversification in terms of underlying assets, solid long-term performance at least on a relative basis, and are trading at decent valuations. Above all, the income goal for the group is to provide a yield of at least 8% or higher.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 08/15/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 13.7% and 16.3% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is good, at nearly -8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Why Is It Important To Have A Diversified CEF Portfolio?

We believe a well-diversified CEF portfolio should contain at least 10 CEFs, preferably from different asset classes. It's also advisable to build the portfolio over a period rather than invest in one lump sum. If you were to invest in one CEF every month for a year, you would have a well-diversified CEF portfolio by the year's end. What we provide here every month is a list of five probable candidates for further research. A CEF portfolio can be an important component of the overall portfolio strategy. One should preferably have a DGI portfolio as the foundation, and the CEF portfolio could be used to boost the income level to the desired level. How much should one allocate to CEFs? Each investor needs to answer this question himself/herself based on their personal situation and factors like the size of the portfolio, income needs, risk appetite, or risk tolerance.

Criteria to Shortlist:

Criteria

Brings down the number of funds to...

Reason for the Criteria

Baseline expense < 2.5% and Avg. Daily Volume > 10,000

Approx. 435 Funds

We do not want funds that charge excessive fees. Also, we want funds that have fair liquidity.

Market capitalization> 100 Million

Approx. 400 Funds

We do not want funds that are too small.

Track record/ History longer than five years (inception date 2016 or earlier)

Approx. 375 Funds

We want funds that have a reasonably long track record.

Discount/Premium < +7%

Approx. 350 Funds

We do not want to pay too high a premium; we want bigger discounts.

Distribution (dividend) Rate > 5%

Approx. 250-290 Funds

The current distribution (income) is reasonably high.

5-Year Annualized Return on NAV > 0% AND/ OR

3-Year Annualized Return on NAV >0%

Approx. 200-250 Funds

We want funds with a reasonably good past track record in maintaining their NAVs.

After we applied the above criteria this month, we are generally left with around 250 funds (plus or minus 5%) on our list. From here on, we apply our other criteria to narrow the list.

Note: All tables in this article have been created by the author (unless explicitly specified). Most of the data in this article are sourced from Cefconnect.com, Cefa.com, and Morningstar.com.

Narrowing Down To 50 Funds

To narrow down the number of funds to a more manageable count, we will shortlist ten funds based on each of the following criteria. Thereafter, we will apply certain qualitative criteria to each fund and rank them to select the top five.

At this stage, we also eliminate certain funds with substantially negative NAV returns for both three-year and five-year periods.

Seven Broad Criteria:

  • Excess discount/premium (explained below).
  • Distribution rate.
  • Return on NAV, last three years (medium-term).
  • Return on NAV, last five years (long term).
  • Coverage ratio.
  • Excess return over distributions.
  • The total weight (calculated up to this point).

Excess Discount/Premium:

We certainly like funds that are offering large discounts (not premiums) to their NAVs. But what's important is to look at the "excess discount/premium" and not the absolute value. We want to see the discount (or premium) relative to their past record, say, a 52-week average.

Excess Discount/Premium = Current Discount/Premium (Minus) 52-Wk Avg. Discount/ Premium.

For more explanation of the excess discount and z-scores, please refer to our blog post (link provided at the beginning of the article).

We sort our list (of approx. 250 funds) on the "excess discount/premium" in descending order. For this criterion, the lower the value, the better it is. So, we select the top 10 funds (with the most negative values) from this sorted list.

(All data as of 08/15/2025)

Table 1:

High Current Distribution Rate:

After all, most investors invest in CEF funds for their juicy distributions. Even though we should not chase yield, at this stage, we want to give them a fair representation in our list and subject them to a more rigorous criterion later on. We sorted our list on the current distribution rate (descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds from this sorted list.

Table 2:

Medium-Term Return on NAV (last three years):

We then sort our list on a three-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and select the top 10 funds. Please note that the huge drawdowns in the case of MLPs during the pandemic (caused by the Oil crash) and subsequent recovery have led to too many MLPs and energy funds topping this list (3-year NAV gains). For this reason, we make a shortlist based on the total score and keep only a few of them on this list.

Table 3:

Five-Year Annualized Return on NAV:

We then sorted our list on the five-year return on NAV (in descending order, highest at the top) and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 4:

Coverage Ratio (Distributions Vs. Earnings):

The coverage ratio is derived by dividing the earnings per share by the distribution amount for a specific period. Please note that in some cases, the coverage ratio may be a bit inaccurate since the "earnings per share" may be three to six months old. But in most cases, it's fairly accurate. Furthermore, the coverage ratio is not critical for pure equity funds as they generate very little dividend income, and they cover most of their distributions from capital gains. However, we sorted our list on coverage ratio and selected the top 10 funds.

Table 5:

Excess Return Over Distribution:

If a fund provides a very high distribution but low returns over 3, 5, or 10-year periods, it does not help the long-term interests of the investor. In simple terms, the fund is just overpaying the distributions and hurting the NAV in the long term. That is why we calculate a factor we like to call "excess return." This excess return is what is provided by the fund over and above the distribution rate over a specified time period. We calculate it by subtracting the distribution rate from the average of the three-year and five-year NAV returns. In this list, we included the top 10 funds.

Table 6:

Total Weight (Quality Score) Calculated Up to This Point:

Note: The Total Weight calculation is not fully completed at this point since we have not considered the 10-year NAV return. However, we select the top 15 names based on current calculations.

Table 7:

From the selections so far, we have 75 funds in total (tables 1-7).

We will need to see if there are any duplicates among them. In our current list of 75 funds, there were 26 duplicates, meaning there are funds that appeared more than once. The following names appear twice (or more):

Appeared twice: CSQ, EFR, EIC, EOD, EOS, IIF, MSD, MXF, PEO, SCD (10 duplicates).

Appeared three times: EMO, KYN, XFLT (6 duplicates).

Appeared four times: PDX, STK (6 duplicates).

Appeared five times: TWN (4 duplicates).

Once we remove 23 duplicate rows, we are left with 49 (75- 26) funds.

Note: It may be worthwhile to mention here that just because a fund has appeared multiple times does not necessarily make it an attractive candidate. Sometimes, a fund may appear multiple times simply for the wrong reasons, like a high current discount, a high excess discount, or a very high distribution rate that may not be sustainable. However, during the second filtering stage, it may not score well on the overall quality score due to other factors like a poor track record. That said, if a fund has appeared four times or more, it may be worth a second look.

Narrowing Down to Just 10 Funds

In our list of funds, we may already have some of the best probable candidates. However, so far, they have been selected based on one (or two) criteria that each may be good at. That's not nearly enough. So, we will apply a combination of criteria by applying weights to eight factors to calculate the total quality score and filter out the best ones.

We will apply weights to each of the eight criteria:

Once we have calculated the weights, we combine them to calculate the "Total Combined Weight," also called the "Quality Score."

The sorted list (spreadsheet) of 49 funds on the "combined total weight or quality score" is attached here.

File-for-export_-_10_Best_CEFs_-_Aug_2025.xlsx

10-Positions Portfolio of The Month

Before we get to the final five, we will narrow down our list to 20 names. So, we sorted our table based on the sector and then the quality score. Furthermore, in this round of selections, we mostly pick one or two top funds from each sector (based on quality score). There is a bit of subjective judgment, as we tend to prioritize funds that pay regular and consistent distributions on a monthly or quarterly basis. We also weed out any fund that may have a very poor track record in terms of NAV performance. Funds that may have inconsistent dividends (even if they are high) generally do not make it to our top list. Furthermore, be aware that many times, single-country funds score high in our rankings, but most of them pay variable dividends on an annual or semi-annual basis. In addition, being single-country funds, they can be inherently riskier since their future returns are tied to just one country, be it economic, regulatory, or geopolitical factors. For these reasons, they generally do not make it to our top 5 lists, but they do appear on our list of 20. Individual investors could consider them based on their goals.

Here's the list of the top 20 selections (from different asset classes) in the order of sector:

Table 8:

10-Best-CEFs of the Month:

So far, we have followed our filtering criteria to a large extent. However, in the end, if we only want to select ten funds, we will need to apply our subjective judgment to some degree. While we narrow down this list to ten names, we would be mindful to keep the list as diversified as possible in terms of sectors and asset classes. Since this step is somewhat subjective, the choice would differ from person to person. Nonetheless, here are the selections for this month, based on our perspective (in order of Quality Score):

Here's the list of the top 10 selections this month (in order of sector):

(STK), (MXF), (SCD), (EOD), (KYN), (BANX), (PEO), (XFLT), (NIE), and (ADX).

Table-9:

Some information about the selections:

We will only briefly describe the selected funds. Since there are 10 of them, we will not be providing a very detailed analysis of them.

Here are some details on our 10 selections:

(STK) Columbia Seligman Prem Technology Growth Fund:

The fund invests in a portfolio of high-growth technology stocks with an option overlay strategy designed to mitigate downside volatility and generate income. The fund writes call options on the NASDAQ 100 Index or its exchange-traded fund equivalent on a month-to-month basis. The aggregate notional amount of the call options could typically range between 40% and 50%. STK's yield is at best moderate at 5.80% for a closed-end fund, but the total returns are highly impressive, even beating many pure-play growth funds.

(MXF) Mexico Fund:

As the name suggests, this is a Mexico country-specific fund and generally invests above 95% of its assets in Mexican securities (or listed on Mexican exchanges). It is primarily an equity fund with over 95% allocation to equities, with the rest in fixed income. The current yield is moderate at 5.5% but the discount is roughly -12%. However, the discount has narrowed somewhat in recent times. It pays distributions on a quarterly basis and raised them by over 13% last month. We recommend not being overweight in any of the country-specific funds.

(SCD) LMP Capital and Income

SCD is a fairly diversified fund invested in equity as well as fixed-income securities from many sectors. However, it has its largest positions in MLPs and technology companies. Besides these two, it has allocations, generally under 10%, to many other sectors like REITs, Financials, and Healthcare.

The fund is categorized under "Global Allocation," but nearly 85% is invested in U.S. securities. Broadly, the current allocation is as follows:

  • 60% Common Stocks, mostly US-based.
  • 15% Energy MLPs
  • 12% REITs equity
  • 12% Preferred and Bond securities

(EOD) Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund:

The fund's primary investment ​goal is to seek a high level of current income, with a secondary goal of long-term growth of capital. The fund is invested roughly 80% in global equities with a focus on dividends, and the rest in fixed income (18%) and cash equivalents (2%). The equity portion is invested in 70-80 companies spread across sectors and regions. The fund also employs an options strategy to enhance income and to generate gains on call options. The fund also uses a modest leverage of roughly 15%.

The allocation to the U.S. is roughly two-thirds of the total at 67% and the rest is invested in countries such as the U.K., France, Japan, China, Germany, Italy, Canada, Taiwan, and Brazil. The largest sector is information Technology at 28%, followed by Financials at 18% and Communications at 11%.

(KYN) Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund:

It is one of the popular MLP funds invested in the midstream companies. The MLP funds have had an incredible run in recent times. They are showing very high returns on a 3-year and 5-year basis due to the energy price crash during the 2020 Covid period. However, they do not score very well on a 10-year return basis. That being said, they are also scoring well in our rankings because of attractive yields and discounts. Currently, KYN is yielding close to 8% and offers nearly a 10% discount. One could also look at another MLP, NB Energy Infra and Income Fund (NML).

(BANX) ArrowMark Financial Corp:

The fund invests in preferred equity, subordinated debt, convertible securities, regulatory capital relief securities, and, to a lesser extent, common equity. The primary objective is to provide a steady, high income and, more or less, a stable NAV.

The fund offers a well-covered distribution that is reasonably high at 8.75% and is fully supported by the NII (net investment income) while maintaining a stable (or growing) NAV. Secondly, it offers exposure to floating rate instruments that have benefited from the rise in interest rates, which may appear to be negative right now. But rates may not come down fast enough. Also, the fund did reasonably well even during the previous zero-interest rate environment, and the management appears to be confident that it can maintain the NII. However, it may be noted that while the fund raised the distribution early this year, it has now lowered it to the same level as in 2024.

(PEO) Adams Natural Resources Fund:

PEO is a great fund to own the major oil companies at a discount (currently -10%). This fund provides very nice and diversified exposure to large energy companies. However, the fund is somewhat concentrated at the top, given the fact that the two oil majors, Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), together represent nearly 37% of the total assets. In addition, it currently provides an attractive yield of close to 10%.

PEO has performed consistently well in our ratings, though the fund prices go up or down based on the oil prices. Please keep in mind that this is an energy sector-specific fund, and hence, one should allocate accordingly.

(XFLT) XAI Octagon FR & Alternative Inc Trust:

This fund is a bit of a controversial choice. The fund is for investors who truly seek very high income. As it is often said, the higher the income yields, the higher the risk. So, please use due diligence. In a portfolio of 10 funds, as a whole, the risk may be contained. Moreover, this fund may balance out our other names that are all heavy on the equity side. The income yield is obviously very attractive at more than 15%. This fund can be used as a means of diversification and elevating the income yield for the overall CEF-based portfolio.

This fund is somewhat complex to understand, but this is how the fund describes itself: "The Trust invests in a dynamically managed portfolio of floating-rate credit instruments and other structured credit investments within the private markets. Under normal market conditions, the Trust will invest at least 80% of managed assets in senior secured loans, CLO debt, and CLO equity."

The fund is currently invested to the extent of 46% in Senior First-Lien Loans, which are considered relatively safer. However, it also invests nearly 50% of its assets in CLOs (Collateralized Loan Obligations) and nearly 3/4th of those in CLO equity tranches, which are considered the riskier part of CLOs. Its closest competitor fund in its space is Eagle Point Credit (ECC).

It is important to note that the fund has cut down the monthly distribution amount twice since December last year. That is also part of the reason that the fund's premium has turned into a sizable discount.

(NIE) Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund:

This fund is a hybrid fund with nearly 60% equity, 30% convertibles, and the rest in bonds and cash. With the kind of asset mix, the fund results in lower volatility than the S&P500, but also slightly lower performance than the S&P500 over the long period of time.

The fund offers an attractive yield of 8.2% but also offers a discount of almost -10%. Also, this fund does not use any leverage, which is an advantage in the current high-interest-rate environment.

(ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund:

This fund regularly appears in our selections under the large-cap U.S. equity category. We feel, under the category of large-cap core dividend funds, this is one of the best funds. The fund has a very long and excellent performance history. The past performance is excellent at 16.8%, 15.1%, and 12.9% on an annualized basis ("NAV") for the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods, respectively. Another plus with ADX is that it uses zero leverage and charges much lower fees compared to its peers.

Sure, there are other funds that are quite attractive, such as the Special Opportunities Fund (SPE) and Liberty All-Star Equity (USA). However, ADX outshines them on a long-term basis.

Some of the top holdings of the fund are similar to those of the S&P500, with many of the Magnificent Seven among them, such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta (META), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Visa (V), and Broadcom (AVGO).

CEF-Specific Investment Risks

It goes without saying that CEFs generally have some additional risks. This section is specifically relevant for investors who are new to CEF investing, but in general, all CEF investors should be aware of it. For more details on these specific risks, please read our blog post on CEFs.

  • Use of high leverage and high fees
  • High volatility (compared to the broader market)
  • Premium over NAVs (market price higher than the NAV)
  • Asset-specific concentration risk.

Concluding Thoughts

The broader market, as represented by the S&P500, provides a dismal yield of less than 1.20%. Many income investors and retirees can't survive on that kind of income. For income investors, we recommend building a core bucket of DGI (Dividend growth) stocks and supplementing it with a basket of CEFs to increase the overall income.

We use our screening process to highlight the best closed-end funds for investment each month. Our filtering process demands that our selections have an above-average long-term performance record, offer an average of roughly 8% distributions, and are relatively cheaper with reasonable discounts. We have also provided a larger list of ten CEFs from many different asset classes.

This month, as a group, the selected 10 best funds are offering an average distribution rate of just under 9% (as of 08/15/2025). Besides excellent distributions, these funds have had an excellent record over the long term and collectively returned 13.7% and 16.3% (annualized) in the last three and five years, respectively. The average discount (to NAV) is good, at nearly -8%. Since this is a monthly series, some selections may overlap from month to month.

Please note that these selections are based on our proprietary rating system and are dynamic in nature. So, they can change from month to month (or even week to week). At the same time, some funds can repeat from one month to the next if they remain attractive over an extended period. Furthermore, note that if a fund has not made it to our list, it does not mean it is not good. It could be because they may not be attractively priced at this time or trading at a premium while running our filtering process.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Consumer Spending Remains Strong
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because, while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market". In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

July data included another decline in housing permits, with a noisy advance in starts. Partly as a result, the index of Leading Indicators declined slightly again. Existing home sales remained rangebound, improving slightly, while their prices are on the verge of declining YoY.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 6.05%, up +0.05% w/w (1-yr range: 5.33-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.26%, down -0.06% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.79%, up +0.11% w/w (1.36-2.02)

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Yield curve

  • 10-year minus 2-year: +0.57%, unchanged w/w (-1.07 - +0.53)
  • 10-year minus 3-month: +0.06%, down -0.04% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2-year minus Fed funds: -0.74%, down -0.16% w/w

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.62%, up +0.06% w/w (6.11-7.26)

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12-month highs of a number of months ago. The spreads between Treasuries and investment-grade and high-yield bonds continue to show little change, having meandered in a relatively narrow range for most of the past several years.

The yield curve measures remain where they have been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2-year spread being the most positive and the 2-year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10-year minus 3-month remained neutral this week.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps up +0.1% at 149 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. down -1 to 147 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +23% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +14% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -3% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +23% [SA]

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.3% YoY (tied for multi-year low)

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY but remain close to their longer-term bottoms, so they score neutral.

In 2023, real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12-month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen, and tied their post-pandemic low once again this week.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. June data was just released four weeks ago:

  • M1 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M1 up +1.6% (3-year high) (worst: -1.4% 8/23)
  • M2 m/m up +0.6%, YoY Real M2 up +1.9% (2.1% June 2025 3-year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23)

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it was either during the latter part of a recession or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (56% actual + 44% estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 29) (no new report until August 29)

  • Q2 up +1.42 to 66.39, up +4.3% q/q

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1, earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2, earnings have rebounded sharply, and as of two weeks ago, the rating has changed from neutral to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index down -.02 (looser) to -0.56 (-0.42 – -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) down -0.01 (looser) to -0.53 (-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.03 (less loose) to -0.44 (+0.13 - -0.76)

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, and a positive number is poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three were recently solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +1 to 396, down -25 m/m (232 8/30/24 - 421 7/25/25) Components: Yield curve 53 Phillips curve 343
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: down -0.2479 to -0.8153 (+0.5569 4/11/25 – -0.9773 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: up +3.5 to +46.0 as of 7/30/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead)

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the "recession-eligible" time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal), which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress Index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and remains among its better post-pandemic readings.

For well over a year, IM has stated that its system does not forecast a recession, although in the last two months, it has edged closer to its threshold.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Unchanged at 120.76 w/w, down -2.0% YoY (last week) (broad) (118.76 – 129.67) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Down -0.12 to 97.74 w/w, down -3.0% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (99.01 – 109.66)

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged and 5% higher YoY. At 5% or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are the positives.

A reminder that I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US dollar as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +1.25 to 101.63 (93.30 - 107.71)
  • Up +5.4% YoY

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx )

Bloomberg Industrial Metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 141.54, up +0.09 w/w (130.47 -173.08 )
  • Down -3.7% YoY

Beginning four months ago, the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into the neutral range. By contrast, the industrial metals measures remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Last week the industrial measure turned back higher YoY, and was so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms but in other major currencies, it is a completely different story. In particular, that industrial commodities are weaker YoY, despite a weaker US$, is definitely not a sign of global strength.

Stock prices: S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up 0.3% to 6466.91

In July and again this month, stocks have made several new all-time highs. As a result, this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Order Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State, up +13.4 to 15.4
  • *Philly down -20.5 to -1.9
  • Richmond down -13 to -25
  • Kansas City up +13 to +2
  • Dallas up +3.7 to -3.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -4 to -2

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these have gradually declined to neutral and then negative. In the past 45 days, these have improved to neutral, and if the average increases just +1 more to +3, this will score as a positive.

In April, I wrote that “I am strongly considering adding the regional service indexes this month, to see if the consumer segment of the economy suddenly shrinks in as close to real time as reasonably available.” Those reports are posted below, with new reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • *Empire State down -2.4 to -11.7
  • *Philly down -30.2 to -0.3
  • Richmond up +6 to -8
  • Kansas City down -5 to -2
  • Dallas up +6.4 to +2.0
  • Average: down -6 to -4

The regional non-manufacturing reports have stabilized at negative levels since May. Meanwhile, the ISM non-manufacturing index, averaged over three months, has continued to weaken. In their latest report released two weeks ago, new orders were negative, and the headline index is only 0.1 above its equipoise point. This means the economically weighted ISM indexes combined showed contraction.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims

  • 235,000, up +11,000 w/w, up +1.3% YoY
  • 4-week average 226,250, up 4,500 w/w, down -3.9% YoY

(Graph at St. Louis FRED)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. They subsequently returned to neutral, then briefly positive, before turning back to neutral. We have had very good reports for the last seven weeks until this week, and four weeks ago, this indicator improved to positive. This appears to be an issue with unresolved seasonality accounting for educational layoffs and rehiring, but it could also be a reflection of non-native born workers in certain industries being deported or being afraid to show up for work, meaning remaining crews are much more tightly held.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link)

  • Up +1 to 88 w/w
  • Down -1.0% YoY (low -13.6%- high -1.5% 8/7/25)

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change, giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $264.2 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $248.0 B last year, up +$16.2 B or +6.5%

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil up +$0.67 to $63.79 w/w, down -7.0% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices up +.01 to $3.13 w/w, down -$0.24 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average down -1.2% YoY

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3-year range, and then turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israel-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased but have since receded back to close to four-year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3-year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, the mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year, it has oscillated between positive and negative.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3-year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.32% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), down -0.02% w/w

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September, so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record for how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • July 2025 vs. July 2024: $29.6 B vs. $8.7 B (+$20.9 B)
  • August 1 - 20 2025 vs. August 1 - 20 2024: $6.5 B vs. $2.1 B (+$4.4 B)

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that the above payments include very little, if anything, from the further tariff hikes that went into effect two weeks ago.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.04 to 2.54 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024)

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024 but generally positive (above 2.00) since then and came very close to setting a new 12-month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in the neutral range, including a new 6-month low, nine weeks ago, it returned to positive territory.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry |

  • Last week: 7-day average up +10% YoY, vs. +10% one week prior

Since 2023, this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general, earlier this year, the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In the past two months, however, this metric trended sharply higher before backing off somewhat last week.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +5.9% YoY, 4-week average +5.8% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index

The Redbook index has been positive since February last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Since then, it had been weakening—until five weeks ago, when it stabilized. Two weeks ago, it increased sharply and has remained very positive since.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.04% to +2.11% YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.06% 9/13/24)

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say, it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons have been turning higher since September but have been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two-month high twelve weeks ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.8% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12-month low 1.7%% 12/16/24)

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads to employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February and then gradually rebounded and made another 12-month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator weakened, although still positive. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads down -0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.7%)
  • Intermodal units down -0.6% YoY (cumulative up +4.4%)
  • Total loads down -0.7% YoY (cumulative up +3.6%)

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report )

Shipping transport

  • Harpex up +18 to 2210 (new 24-month high)
  • Baltic Dry Index down -146 to 1893

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July, and remained positive—until this week.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12-month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year, its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year but rebounded all the way to the top of its six-month range earlier this year before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production ( American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Down -1.4% w/w
  • Up +2.8% YoY

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently, it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week, its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and five weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long-leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10-year Treasury ✓
10-year – 2-year Treasury ✓
10-year – 3 month Treasury ✓
2 year-Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 6 3 5
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities x ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims ✓
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 8 3 3
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail x ✓
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 8 1 1

.

There were a few changes to any of the indicators this week. The long leading indicators continue to be very mixed, while the short leading and coincident indicators remain very positive this week, despite several negative component changes.

To reiterate a comment I made last week, a number of the short-leading and coincident indicators are tied to the value of the US$, including commodities and shipping prices. Normally, prices are mainly rated based on demand, so an increase in price means an increase in economic activity.

I continue to believe that consumer spending and initial jobless claims are the two most important high-frequency indicators at present, and both remain well behaved.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street this week was headed for a solid loss, weighed down by a rout in technology stocks and disappointing retail earnings. However, a dovish signal from Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell on Friday at the annual Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium sparked a big rally, helping the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) eke out gains for the week.

While the S&P and also the blue-chip Dow (DJI) notched weekly gains, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) posted a decline, as cautious investors ahead of Jackson Hole sold off pricey megacap technology stocks. Meanwhile, retail giants Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT) slid after their quarterly results, with the latter also naming a new chief executive.

Anticipation ahead of Powell's speech was high, with market participants hoping for comments on easing monetary policy. Though Powell did not fully commit to cutting interest rates, he noted that the downside risks to the labor market were increasing and that the "baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance."

For the week, the S&P (SP500) added +0.3%, while the Dow (DJI) gained +1.5%. The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) slipped -0.6%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

ONEOK (OKE) - My Highest Conviction Energy Infra C-Corp.

Load Up On ConocoPhillips (COP) While The Market Ignores.

QXO (QXO) Is Ambitious, But It's Executing Sensibly So Far.

Deckers Outdoor (DECK) Has Become An Irresistible Bargain.

I Was Wrong About JEPQ, The Yield Provides A Decent Buffer.

I Called Credo's (CRDO) Rally, Here's Why I'm Downgrading It.

data.title: LyondellBassell: Dividend Investors Proceed With Caution, High Probability Of A Dividend Cut
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past few years, the macro environment has placed downward pressure on businesses, mostly due to rising costs as a result of higher interest rates to combat inflation.

Some industries have been impacted more so than others, leading to a steep decline in their share prices. One of those has been LyondellBasell Industries (LYB), a chemical company that has a long operating history dating back to the 1950's and operations all over the globe.

Despite downward pressure on their cash flows, management seems committed to maintaining its current dividend, which I believe could be in jeopardy of being cut in the near future.

In this article, I discuss LyondellBasell's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why, despite their current attractive dividend yield of 10%, investors should proceed with caution as a dividend cut could be in the not-too-distant future.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, LYB has a long history dating back to the 1950's, going public only 15 years ago. The company operates in six segments and manufactures products for a wide array of applications like food, transportation, and healthcare to name a few.

Their segments include: Olefins & Polyolefins - Americas, Olefins & Polyolefins - Europe, Asia, & International, Intermediates & Derivatives, Advanced Polymer Solutions, Technology, & Refining.

Latest Quarter

LyondellBasell Industries reported their Q2 earnings at the beginning of the month with a large miss on their bottom line. EPS amounted to $0.62, missing analysts' estimates by a sizable $0.16. The miss was also a testament to the headwinds the chemical industry has faced in recent years.

And LyondellBasell's share price has been a direct reflection of this, down over 43% at the time of writing. And this is because of the steep decline in financials the chemical maker has experienced year-over-year, a result of persistent headwinds like rising energy & feedstock costs.

The decline in their bottom line was significant as the previous year's quarter saw EPS of $2.24, more than a 72% decline, offsetting the increase from $0.33 in the prior quarter.

Not surprisingly, sales also suffered a steep decline as the sector has been plagued by rising costs as well as weakness in construction & automotive.

This is also evident by the significant decline their peer Dow Inc. (DOW) saw in their top and bottom lines, although not as steep. Dow experienced a 38% EPS decline year-over-year from $0.68 to $0.42, while their sales also declined 9.0%.

LYB's sales retracted double-digits from $10.558 billion the previous year's quarter to approximately $7.66 billion. However, their segments did see positives in the quarter due to lower costs, which slightly improved margins. But investors/main question is likely: How safe is the dividend?

Deteriorating Cash Flows

As solely a dividend-focused investor, cash flows are vital during my research. While many investors pay attention to a company's earnings payout ratio, the free cash flow payout ratio should be the preferred metric to look at when assessing dividend safety. But with the large decline in earnings, LYB's earnings payout ratio is well-above 100%.

However, management remains committed to the dividend with the 2.2% raise in May to $1.37 a share, currently putting the earnings payout ratio above 150%.

Free cash flow has continued erode since 2021 as a result of headwinds. Since the end of 2021, free cash flow has declined from approximately $7 billion to $2 billion in 2024. And this has been a result of cash from operations declining.

At the end of 2021, cash from operations was $7.6 billion, while free cash flow amounted to $5.7 billion. At the end of 2024, cash from operations declined to $3.8 billion, while free cash flow was $2 billion.

And while LyondellBasell sufficiently covered the dividend last year, their free cash flow payout ratio has deteriorated as a result of the decline. Paying out $1.7 billion in dividends in 2024, their free cash flow payout ratio of 85% gave them little room for error.

Moreover, in the most recent quarter cash flows have continued to decline. Cash from operations stood at just $359 million, while they paid out $536 million in dividend and buybacks.

Although, this was an improvement was the prior quarter that saw negative cash flows due to additional tax payments. Year-to-date, cash from operations was $230 million, down significantly from $1.2 billion last year.

Capital Allocation Strategy

Going forward, management stated they don't plan to repurchase any additional shares for the remainder of the year and for 2026. Aside from this, management has already reduced CAPEX by putting certain growth projects on hold.

This year they expect CAPEX to be $200 million lower, now expected to be $1.7 billion, driving incremental cash flow growth of $600 million. They also expect to reduce additional operations costs and sell some of their European assets next year. And while these are all great efforts, macro uncertainty should keep investors cautious.

Can The Balance Sheet Support Dividend Sustainability?

During the quarter, management seemed optimistic of their dividend sustainability, one reason being due to their available liquidity. This amounted to $1.7 billion in cash on hand for a total of $6.35 billion including revolver capacity.

And it seems as if cash has been used to sustain the dividend so far. Cash on hand at the end of 2024 was $3.4 billion, while leverage stood at 1.8x. During the quarter net debt to EBITDA was 3.2x, so their leverage ratio has been rising and is something investors should keep a close eye on going forward. But the question is: How much cash will they burn through to sustain the dividend?

Tariffs could also show up in the back half of the year. If so, this could cause costs to rise further, forcing management to cut the dividend, similar to peer Dow Inc, which I discussed last month in an article you can read here.

Valuation

As a result of the sharp price decline in the past year, LyondellBasell's current yield sits at 10% at the time of writing which investors may consider attractive.

But using their earnings projection of $2.69 for the year, their forward P/E of 20.29x still seems expensive for a company facing a plethora of headwinds and at high risk of a dividend cut.

I agree with Wall Street's current hold rating and think investors should wait to see sequential improvements in cash flows before considering investing here.

Risks & Conclusion

As a result of current headwinds, LyondellBasell Industries is a high-risk stock that could suffer more downside, especially if they cut the dividend.

While I do believe management is committed to the dividend, showing confidence with the recent increase in May, further economic weakening could force their hand despite their capital allocation strategy.

And while lower interest rates will likely benefit them, macro uncertainty and current cash flow issues leads me to believe they have a high probability of seeing a dividend cut in the next 6 to 12 months.

If so, they could see further downside. As a result of LyondellBassell's high chances of a dividend cut and macro uncertainty, I rate them a hold here.

The performance data shown represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Returns for periods shorter than one year are cumulative, and results for other share classes will vary. Shares will fluctuate in value and, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance cited. Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The second quarter began with a shock to financial markets: a U.S. tariff policy announcement that was significantly more aggressive than expected. Volatility spiked, stock prices plunged, bond yields fell across the yield curve, and credit spreads widened.

Within a week, however, the U.S. administration changed tack with a 90-day pause on new tariffs, providing a much-needed reprieve for the markets. Stock prices sharply rebounded and ended the quarter at a record high. Meanwhile,

U.S. Treasury yields rose, and by the end of June, credit spreads had narrowed significantly.

As of quarter end, corporate earnings expectations declined while the valuation of the stock market based on expected earnings increased. Trade remained a source of uncertainty, as investors assessed the impact of tariffs on inflation, economic growth, and the U.S. Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy. Combined with increased geopolitical tensions and uncertainty surrounding future rate cuts, we believe this backdrop warrants additional caution.

Despite the uncertain market environment, we expect the outperformance opportunity to be greater from individual security selection than from general market exposure. Given highly fluid near-term sentiment, our strategy is to maintain a steady, long-term outlook.

The fund’s equity portfolio outperformed due to favorable security selection across most sectors, especially in information technology. In contrast, sector positioning, which derives from our bottom-up security selection process, was negative, especially in healthcare and energy.

Individually, the largest relative contributor this quarter was Broadcom, Inc., a semiconductor company whose shares rose amid optimism about the company’s opportunity to benefit from the burgeoning AI market. Confidence in spending on AI also lifted shares of a second fund holding, chipmaker Micron Technology, Inc. A third relative contributor was the fund’s relative underweight in consumer electronics company Apple, Inc., a poor performer this quarter that was sold from the portfolio before period end.

In contrast, an underweight in AI chipmaker NVIDIA Corp. detracted, as the company’s shares rose sharply this period. Another performance challenge was an overweight in drugmaker Eli Lilly & Company, whose stock lagged amid uncertainty about drug pricing, including potential tariffs on pharmaceutical products. Lacking exposure to strong-performing benchmark component Meta Platforms, Inc., parent company of Facebook and Instagram, also contributed.

For the fixed-income portfolio, which delivered a positive return that outperformed the index, an out-of-benchmark allocation to high-yield bonds contributed, as did smaller overweights in investment-grade corporate bonds and other credit sectors. However, a sizable overweight in mortgage-backed securities detracted.

Contributors and detractors

The fund’s equity portfolio benefited from strong stock selection across a range of sectors, with notable outperformance in consumer discretionary and technology. Our bottom-up approach to security selection was especially effective in identifying opportunities within the software and semiconductor industries.

Among individual holdings, the top relative contributor this period was Alphabet Inc., whose shares gained on solid advertising revenues and continued strength in cloud services. Another key contributor was a leading electric vehicle manufacturer, which rebounded sharply on better-than-expected delivery volumes. Additionally, our position in a global payments company added value, as it benefited from increased transaction volumes and positive earnings momentum.

Conversely, the portfolio’s underweight in a leading energy firm detracted, as the stock rallied with rising oil prices. A holding in a regional bank also underperformed, driven by weaker loan growth and regulatory concerns. Exposure to a consumer staples company was another negative, as the company faced pricing pressure and softer demand.

Within the fixed-income segment, performance was modestly positive. Outperformance was driven by an underweight to long-duration Treasuries, which lagged amid rising rates. Tactical allocations to corporate bonds and emerging market debt also contributed. However, a concentrated position in agency mortgage-backed securities weighed on results as spreads widened late in the period.

Portfolio changes

The fund’s equity and fixed-income allocations finished June at about 57% and 43%, respectively, compared with 58% and 42%, respectively, on March 31.

In the equity portfolio, we established four new positions and sold two stocks from the fund. Turnover remained somewhat elevated, reflecting various attractive valuation opportunities we saw in Q2.

In the fixed-income portfolio, we increased the fund’s allocation to agency mortgage-backed securities, with smaller additions in asset-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities. We funded these moves by reducing the portfolio’s weightings in investment-grade corporate bonds and U.S. Treasuries.

Footnotes

1 Listed holdings reflect the largest portions of the fund’s total and may change at any time. They are not recommendations to buy or sell any security. Data is expressed as a percentage of net assets and excludes cash and cash equivalents.

2 It is not possible to invest in an index. Index definitions can be found within the "What you should know before Investing" section on the last page of this fact sheet.

3“Net (what you pay)” represents the effect of a contractual fee waiver and/or expense reimbursement and is subject to change.

  • Dividend Growth stocks outperformed dividend cutters by 253 times over the 50 years.

And that brings us to the question that Nvidia investors want to know.
